INSPECTOR’S ASSESSMENT
General
49. The coastline and cliffs in the Plémont area in the north-west corner of the
island are unique. The dramatic and rugged landscape, tall north facing
cliffs, wildlife, and the now largely absent presence of modern buildings and
man-made structures, combine to create a character that is wild and
beautiful. These very special qualities are reflected in BIP designations
(PCA, CNP, and MZ) which combine to, rightly, create a highly protective
planning policy regime, i.e., it is not a place where most, or indeed any,
forms of development would normally be considered appropriate.
50. It is therefore unsurprising that the appeal proposal, which would introduce
change to this natural landscape, has become the focus of divergent views.
Although the parties differ in terms of the weighting they apply, it is a
matter of common ground that the proposed fence will cause some visual
landscape harm. Indeed, it would be difficult to argue that introducing a
near 1 kilometre long metal fence into this wild and natural landscape
setting would not cause some degree of visual detriment. The crux of this
appeal is whether the proposal is justified by the evidence and whether it
conforms with the BIP.
51. For the purposes of the Hearing sessions, it was agreed that the grounds of
appeal covered the following 4 topics:
Ground 1 – Evidence
Ground 2 – Planning policies
Ground 3 – Planning conditions
Ground 4 – POA
52. I set out my assessment below following the same order as the Hearing
sessions, accepting that there is a degree of overlap between the respective
grounds, before drawing conclusions.
Evidence
53. The appellant’s principal challenge is that historic and scientific evidence
does not demonstrate a cause-and-effect relationship between the decline
in seabird numbers and the effects of non-native predators (which the fence
seeks to control) and, without that evidential rationale, the proposal can
only result in harm and should be refused.
54. At the Hearing, I did explain that a Planning Inspector is not expected to
become an instant expert on the efficacy of seabird conservation measures,
but it is my role to weigh the evidence before me in a logical and objective
manner.
55. My review finds that the evidence submitted in support of the application is
voluminous and wide ranging. It includes detailed research studies and
11

reports, along with submissions from conservation bodies from different
countries offering support for the project.
56. The principal research studies, in chronological order of publication, include:
Seabird Monitoring Around Plémont – Project Puffin Jersey by Sangan Island
Conservation (2017) which recorded small numbers of puffins and razorbills,
as well as other seabirds; Options for the Recovery of Nesting Seabirds on
Jersey, Channel Islands by KJ Swinnerton, HG Young, and P Sangan (2018)
which explored the decline in seabird numbers and possible response
measures; Plémont Seabird Reserve Feasibility Study Report for a Predator-
Exclusion Fence by Christina Sellares de Pedro MSc (2021), which assessed
the feasibility and efficacy of a (much longer) fence proposal; Preliminary
Ecological Appraisal Report Plémont Seabird Sanctuary by Dr Amy Louise
Hall of Sangan Island Conservation (2024) which assessed the ecological
impacts of the proposal; Plémont Seabird Sanctuary Biodiversity Research
2017–2024 by Christina Sellares de Pedro MSc (2024); Case for the Plémont
Seabird Sanctuary by Christina Sellares de Pedro MSc (2024) which
presents the case for the fence to create an ‘eco-sanctuary’ free of invasive
predators.
57. I have reviewed each of these documents and I note that the respective
authors appear to be highly experienced, qualified and experts in their
fields. I have paid particular attention to the overarching review and
assessment undertaken by Professor Atkinson, who was engaged to provide
independent advice to the planning authority, given his professional
expertise concerning seabird conservation, including within the Channel
Islands, and his senior role at the British Trust for Ornithology.
58. He assesses the research work undertaken to be comprehensive and
convincing, and that the area is currently in a nature depleted state. He
further advises that: "There is sufficient evidence from the other Channel
Islands of Guernsey, Sark and Alderney, the Isles of Scilly and the island of
Lundy in the Bristol Channel to show that (a) seabird populations are
increasing elsewhere, (b) that there is a likely pool of young non-breeding
birds that are looking for suitable breeding sites to recruit into and (c) that
predator removal elsewhere has resulted in stunning increase in breeding
seabirds. If successfully implemented, then the chances of success are
high."
59. With regard to other evidence, I have read and noted the range of
submissions from other bodies and organisations expressing support for the
project and its approach. Whilst these do not carry the same weight as
expert research and surveys, it amounts to an impressive canon of support
from reputable and experienced conservation bodies and governmental
departments around the globe. That said, I do agree with the appellant’s
submission that what may be an appropriate conservation measure in one
part of the world, such as Hawaiʻi, is not necessarily appropriate elsewhere.
I have weighted this evidence accordingly. I have also taken into account
the more anecdotal evidence concerning the presence of rats originally
linked with the holiday camp operation, and accounts of rats being present
close to the sea and taking fisherman’s bait.
12

60. The appellant has considerable local knowledge and experience and remains
unconvinced by the evidential basis of the proposal. However, this stance
appears to be premised on the view that the evidence base has to provide
complete, or at least very high levels of, certainty that predators are the
main problem, that the fence will be effective in preventing predator ingress
and that seabird populations will increase, particularly those most
vulnerable, i.e. puffins and razorbills.
61. In my view, that would be unrealistic and it does rather highlight an area
where the opposing appeal parties may actually find some level of
agreement. In this regard, it is the case that declines in seabird numbers
may have multiple causes, not all of which may be fully understood within
the current body of empirical research. That is not unusual in the natural
environment, where species numbers will be the subject of a wide range of
environmental factors, and I note the appellant’s submission that his
experience as a fisherman suggests that the decline in puffin numbers over
more than a century may be linked to changes in the available food supply
of small fish, which may have moved the bird population northwards,
although I am not aware of any research study that supports that theory.
62. It is also the case that the proposed fence may not be 100% effective and,
at the Hearing, the applicant explained the concept of a ‘leaky fence’, and
maintained that, with good management, it will be very effective. Moreover,
the applicant accepts that success is not 100% guaranteed and, indeed, has
volunteered to accept a time limited permission and to enter a legal
obligation to decommission the fence, at some considerable cost, should it
prove to be ineffective.
63. I have also considered carefully the appellant’s concerns and allegations
about equipment placements on land without owners’ consent, and the
applicant’s submission that, to the best of its knowledge, appropriate
landowner permissions were in place. Whilst not wishing to downplay any
unauthorised access to land, if it had occurred, these are civil matters that
are not pivotal to my overall planning assessment.
64. Rounding up all of the above leads me to a finding that there is a solid and
weighty evidential foundation supporting the proposal in principle. I am
satisfied that the evidence demonstrates that invasive predators are highly
likely to be a key factor in the decline of seabird populations in this locality,
and that the proposed predator fence is an appropriate and potentially
effective conservation response to that problem. My finding here is simply
one concerning whether the evidence base supports the project in principle,
and I find that it does. That does not automatically lead to a finding that
planning permission should be granted. I examine the planning policy
implications under the remaining grounds.
65. On this ground, I conclude that the appeal should not succeed.
13

Planning policies
66. The combination of BIP designations (PCA, CNP, and MZ) and BIP policies
designed to protect the natural environment, the landscape and seascape
character, island identity, and key views, mean that planning controls on
new development are some of the most stringent on the island.
67. In layperson’s terms, these designations and policies could be interpreted as
a virtual ban on modern development and that the existing state of wildness
and natural beauty of the landscape should always prevail. However, the
BIP policies are more complex and nuanced than what might be seen as a
near total moratorium on new development, and certain types of
development are permissible under specified circumstances, notably where
there is a predicted public benefit which outweighs any harm, and that harm
has been minimised as far as is reasonably practical. Moreover, whilst some
policies seek to protect and control visual and physical impacts, other
policies, and indeed some elements of the same policies, promote and
encourage biodiversity and green infrastructure protections and
enhancements.
68. The result is a complex policy backcloth of pushes and pulls, and, as
recognised in the BIP’s introduction3, it is important for the decision maker
to consider the plan as a whole and to carefully balance the planning merits
of a development proposal with the policy requirements of the plan. This
assessment links closely to the ground 1 considerations of whether the
evidence base supports the proposal and provides a justification for any
harms that may be identified.
69. In assessing harm, the logical starting point is to turn to the JILSCA, which
informed the BIP, and the associated Landscape and Seascape Character
Guidance SPG (July 2023). The JILSCA places this area within the ‘Cliffs and
Headlands’ landscape type which, in summary4, ‘comprises the rugged
coastal areas of Jersey, including some of the island’s best-known scenery.
It is often elevated, and includes cliffs (down to the high water mark), rock
faces, heath, coastal grassland and occasional patches of coastal woodland
and scrub’ and that ‘it contains some of Jersey’s wildest and most remote
areas. It is highly valued for its spectacular coastal views, its rich
biodiversity and geodiversity, and its sense of isolation and tranquillity.’
70. When judged against this character assessment, the notion of a near 1
kilometre length of 2 metres high metal fencing with associated posts,
foundations, capping and gates, snaking around the headland area, along
with the proposed areas either side of the fence that would be managed to
prevent the growth of higher vegetation, does not sit entirely comfortably in
visual and landscape terms. It would be a man-made and alien structure at
odds with the character descriptors of remoteness, ruggedness and natural
wildness.
3 Pages 3 – 4 Bridging Island Plan (2022)
4 Page 38 - Jersey Integrated Landscape and Seascape Character Assessment (2020)
14

71. I am mindful here that, other than the coastal path itself and the remnants
of historic buildings and structures which have blended with and become
part of the landscape, there is very little in the way of man-made
interventions along the proposed route of the fence. The sense of natural
beauty and isolation are very real and tangible, and they are key factors in
defining the area’s special character and qualities.
72. The design function of the structure, to exclude non-native predators
including rats and feral ferrets which can dig, burrow and climb,
necessitates certain specifications and engineering if it is to be effective.
These include metal mesh, skirting, and capping, along with managed strips
either side of the fence where vegetation would be kept low. These
requirements mean that the scope to soften and screen the fence are
limited. However, I discuss the issue of materials and finish under ground 3
later in this report.
73. Understandably, the Government’s Landscape Officer has recorded an
objection5 to the proposal and sets out her view that ‘there would be an
adverse visual impact in both short and long-range views experienced from
the land and the sea’ and that this is ‘considered to challenge policies SP4,
HE1 and NE3’. These policies, respectively, seek to protect and promote
island identity, the historic environment, and landscape and seascape
character. To a degree I concur with this assessment, although I note that
the Historic Environment Team consultation response was one of ‘no
objection’, with regard to policies HE1 and SP4.
74. The Landscape Officer does also recognise that an ecological need may
amount to an overriding public benefit (that may justify the identified
harm). She also assesses that the project would make available resources
to manage and remove bracken found to dominate the area, to allow a
greater planting diversity and enhancement of grassland and heathland
habitats, and that this would be measurably beneficial to the local landscape
character.
75. With regard to harm minimisation, the applicant submits, and the planning
authority agrees, that it has sought to minimise impacts as far as is
reasonably practicable. The alignment of the proposed fence, being well
below the coastal path, means that, along much of its length, it will not be a
particularly conspicuous feature and the natural beauty of the area will
prevail in the viewer’s eye. That will also be the case when viewed from the
sea, as the alignment means that the fence will be seen against the
backcloth of rising land, and it will not, for example, break the skyline when
viewed from a vessel in mid and longer range views.
76. However, I assess that there are locations, in shorter and longer distance
views, where the fence will be more visible and unwelcome in visual
amenity terms. In shorter views, based on my site inspection and
assessment, I consider that the fence would be most imposing where it
would loop inland around Le Creux Gros where a stream flows down a valley
to the sea; this is shown on the applicant’s photomontage imagery for
5 Landscape Officer consultation response dated 20 December 2024
15

‘viewpoint 10’. Similarly, in longer distance views, viewpoints 8 and 9
provide examples of how the linear man-made intervention will appear to
jar somewhat against the otherwise untouched natural landscape.
77. Turning to the specific BIP policies in more detail, I will begin by exploring
the NE policies first and then other related policies.
78. With regard to policy NE1, this seeks to protect and improve biodiversity
and geodiversity; it states that all development must ensure that the
importance of habitats, designated sites and species is taken into account
and should seek to improve biodiversity and geodiversity value and, where
possible, to deliver biodiversity net gain. The appeal proposal is, in essence,
a biodiversity protection and enhancement project, aimed specifically at
seabirds, some species of which have declined in numbers and are now
rare. Whilst I appreciate that the appellant will disagree, my findings on
ground 1 lead me to believe that the evidence base underpinning this
project is fundamentally sound, and its objectives are matters of important
public benefit, and, in this regard, I am mindful of representations in
support of the project from islanders. I assess that the proposal accords
with policy NE1 and that this should attract significant weight.
79. Concerning policy NE2, this supports the protection and enhancement of
green infrastructure and biodiversity. I am mindful here of the research
reports, and the convincing summary provided by Professor Atkinson, which
evidence the nature depleted state of the headland areas, and benefits
predicted by predator exclusion and land management measures, notably
through the removal of invasive bracken. In seeking to protect and improve
the green infrastructure to benefit seabirds, which are integral to the
island’s biodiversity, and thereby maximise their successful nesting and
chick rearing potential, the proposal accords with policy NE2. Again, this
policy compliance attracts significant weight.
80. With regard to policy NE3, this emphasizes the protection of the island’s
landscape and seascape character. It is an important policy to consider in
this case, because it not only requires development to protect or improve
landscape and seascape character, but it also sets the bar at the ‘highest
level of protection’ within the PCA and the CNP. However, with specific
reference to the CNP, it links the compatibility of development with the
purposes of the park, namely (i) the conservation and enhancement of the
natural beauty, wildlife and cultural heritage of the park, and (ii) the public
understanding and enjoyment of its special qualities. This leads to the need
for a sophisticated and balanced assessment, because the acknowledged
visual harm to ‘natural beauty’ of the landscape arising from the proposed
man-made fence structure is entirely a product of a project designed to
enhance the wildlife of the CNP, which, in turn, is part of its intrinsic beauty
and undoubtedly contributes to the public’s understanding and enjoyment of
its special qualities.
81. There is therefore a push and pull within the first half of policy NE3 in this
particular case. In my view, based on the evidence before me, this leads to
a relatively neutral outcome, i.e., there will be some limited landscape
character harm, but it is offset by its predicted wildlife benefits. However,
16

should the Minister calibrate this differently, and assess that the landscape
and seascape harm to be greater, the second part of policy NE2 would come
into play. This does allow for scenarios where a development does not
protect or improve landscape character, provided exception criteria are met.
In my assessment, those tests would be met, because the project has been
evidenced to be necessary in the public interest, there is no reasonable
alternative, harm has been mitigated as far as is reasonably practicable,
and the predicted benefit outweighs any identified harm. Moreover, the
applicant’s support for a temporary 15-year permission and the entering of
a POA with decommissioning obligations, provide additional comfort that
policy NE3 objectives will be secured in the longer term, even if the project
were to be unsuccessful with regard to its primary purpose.
82. Turning to other policies cited by the appellant, there are connecting
threads and overlaps with the NE policies explored above.
83. SP4 is a high-level strategic policy which requires development to protect
and promote island identity. It highlights the importance of preserving
Jersey’s unique identity, including its natural and cultural heritage. In my
view, whilst acknowledging some landscape and visual harm, the proposal
would support island identity as a place of ecological significance by
protecting seabirds which have been in evidenced decline. Some species of
seabird, notably the Atlantic Puffin, are iconic in Jersey culture and a source
of publicly acknowledged concern due to their decline in numbers. I find no
conflict with policy SP4.
84. SP5 addresses protecting and improving the natural environment and
provides the strategic policy content for matters included within other
policies, including NE1, NE2 and NE3. I reach similar findings in this regard,
and I find no fundamental tensions with this policy, as the proposal is
designed to improve the natural environment, which policy SP5 supports.
85. Places policy PL5 seeks to protect or improve the character and
distinctiveness of the countryside, coast and marine environment, including
the PCA and CNP. My findings under the other policies set out above leads
me to the conclusion that the proposal is compatible with the area and the
purposes of the CNP and I find no tension with policy PL5.
86. Policy GD9 addresses Skyline, Views, and Vistas and requires development
proposals to avoid causing harm to strategic views, vistas, and landmarks.
Again, this overlaps with earlier policy assessment. However, it is worth
noting that the siting of the fence avoids skyline impacts and does not
impact on any identified strategic views. It will, however, be visible in some
views which are currently unspoiled and it will cause some limited harm.
However, in line with other policies, I have assessed that the
community/public benefit outweighs any limited harm, and therefore policy
GD9 is satisfied.
87. In conclusion on planning policies, I assess the proposal to be in conformity
with the relevant BIP policies and that this ground of appeal should
therefore not succeed.
17

Planning conditions
88. In addition to the standard time limit and plans compliance conditions (A
and B), the Decision Notice incudes 6 further conditions. The appellant has
challenged conditions 2, 5, and 6. However, for completeness, I will provide
my assessment of all 6 conditions in terms of their compliance with the
tests usually applied for planning conditions, namely that they should be
necessary, relevant to planning, relevant to the development, enforceable,
precise, and reasonable.
89. Condition 1 time limits the permission to 15 years, with the reason stating
that this is to allow the Chief Officer to reassess the impact and give further
consideration at that time. Based on the evidence and linked to the POA
(see below) this condition is reasonable. The time period is a matter of
judgement, but feels to me to strike the right balance in allowing sufficient
time to prove the project’s success (or failure) and the acknowledged
landscape harm through its visual impact. The effect of the condition does
not mean that the fence would be automatically removed at the end of the
15 year period. However, it does signal that, if the project were to be a
complete failure, there would be little likelihood of a further permission.
Equally, if the project were to be an undisputed success, with increased
numbers of rare breeding seabirds, a further permission is likely to be
granted, all other things being equal.
90. Condition 2 requires the fencing to be painted in a brown/green finish to be
approved in writing by the Chief Officer and maintained as such thereafter.
The appellant submits that the requirement to apply a paint finish to
stainless steel mesh fence (condition 2) is not practical in this location, and
the applicant also states that it is not its intention to apply a paint finish. I
agree and consider that, whilst the posts may be finished in a neutral
colour, it would not be practical to paint the mesh fencing and, even if it
were, the exposure to weather would likely result in deterioration over time.
At the Hearing, the applicant produced a photograph of a naturally oxidised
predator fence (from Hawaiʻi) which, in my view, seems a more appropriate
approach than painting. This matter can be addressed by a less prescriptive
wording, and this is reflected in my recommendation at the end of this
report.
91. Conditions 3 and 4 relate to requirements for archaeological oversight and
procedures in the event of finds of historic features. These are uncontested,
reasonable and necessary conditions in terms of the BIP’s historic
environment policy provisions (notably SP4, HE1 and HE5).
92. Condition 5 requires the submission and approval of a 15 year duration
LEMP before development begins. It states that the LEMP must be based on
the approved Landscape Management Plan and must cover a wide range of
matters. These include evaluation of the site's existing landscape, habitats,
and species; assessment of potential impacts of the development;
mitigation measures; a schedule of landscape works; proposals for
ecological enhancement; an implementation timetable; a 15-year
management and maintenance plan; a monitoring and evaluation
framework to assess the success of measures, including targets,
18

methodologies, and reporting mechanisms; and measures to identify and
control invasive non-native species. Whilst I note that the appellant says
that the department has no effective way to ensure that the applicant
abides by the LEMP, there is no evidence before me to suggest that it would
not, or that the planning authority could not use its planning compliance
powers in the event of any breach of the condition. In my assessment, this
condition is necessary, enforceable and extremely important, as it ensures
that the predicted benefits of the development are properly managed,
delivered and given the highest chances of success.
93. Condition 6 requires a Gate Access Management and Agreement Plan to be
submitted and approved before development begins, detailing gate
locations, access protocols, maintenance responsibilities, and emergency
provisions, while ensuring security and wildlife protection. Whilst the
appellant challenges the effectiveness of such a plan and how it would
operate in practice, the requirement is a sensible and necessary measure to
balance ecological protection with other real world logistical issues.
94. In conclusion on conditions, subject to some rewording of condition 2, I
endorse the 6 conditions, which meet the necessary accepted tests.
Planning Obligations Agreement
95. The minutes of the May 2025 Planning Committee record that ‘The
Committee also requested that a Planning Obligation Agreement be entered
into, to ensure the ongoing maintenance of the fence and its surrounds, as
well as the eventual decommissioning of the fence.’ The POA was prepared
and ultimately signed on 23 October 2025, the 2 signatories being the Chief
Officer for the Environment and ‘The Public of the Island’ (c/o Jersey
Property Holdings, Government of Jersey, Union Street, St Helier).
96. The appellant has expressed concern about the POA, stating his expectation
that BOTE would be a party to the legal agreement, and claims that the
document as signed would place ‘an approx. £6 million cost’ of
decommissioning onto the public purse.
97. At the Hearing it was explained to me that the signatories are simply a
product of the legal facts of land ownership. Mr Le Maistre (NTJ) advised me
that decommissioning costs would be nowhere near those quoted and that
the public purse was not exposed to any risk. He advised that the States
had complete control through the required submission of decommissioning
strategy and any property lease/licence arrangements to ensure that project
funds would be in place to address any future decommissioning liability.
98. Whilst I am satisfied with the explanation provided from a planning
perspective, these are matters that stray into landlord and tenant (or
similar) arrangements, and the Minister will need to be satisfied that
appropriate financial safeguards are in place should the project proceed.
19

OTHER MATTERS
Historic environment
99. I have read the Heritage Impact Statement (September 2024) submitted
with the application, which concludes that there would be a minor negative
impact on the settings of 4 Listed Buildings in the vicinity, but, because
there is no alternative means to deliver this proposal and its goals without
the installation of the fence, and the negative impact has been mitigated
and reduced through considerate design and siting, there could be an
overriding public benefit in meeting other environmental policy objectives.
100. I have also studied the archaeological Desk Based Assessment which
established that there is archaeological interest within the site, in particular
with regard to deposits relating to the WWII German occupation, and also
some potential archaeology from the prehistoric era.
101. I have noted the Historic Environment Team’s ‘no objection’ response and I
agree that, subject to a safeguarding condition regarding archaeology, the
proposal is acceptable in terms of historic environment impacts and
associated BIP policies.
CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATION
102. This appeal concerns the decision to grant planning permission for the
erection of a 907 metre long predator exclusion fence at Plémont, St. Ouen,
to create a seabird sanctuary. The appellant, Mr. K. Journeaux, has
challenged the decision, arguing that the proposal is based on flawed
scientific evidence, would cause environmental and visual harm, that some
of the planning conditions are defective and that the POA imposes an undue
financial burden on the public.
103. The appeal raises some quite complex planning judgements and balances.
Indeed, it is a good case study in the policy pushes and pulls contained
within the BIP, and it underlines the sophisticated balancing exercise that
the decision maker must undertake. Most notably the balance concerns
whether to allow some visual and landscape harm as a result of the
proposal, to achieve the predicted benefits of seabird and habitat
protections and enhancements.
104. My assessment finds that the evidence supporting the proposal is
comprehensive and credible, demonstrating that invasive predators are
likely a key factor, although not the only factor, in the decline of seabird
populations in the area. The proposed fence, while not guaranteed to be
100% effective, is a reasonable conservation measure supported by
international experts and organisations, with the approach achieving proven
successes in other places. The project’s temporary nature and the POA
provide safeguards for decommissioning if the project proves unsuccessful.
105. My assessment acknowledges that the fence would cause some visual and
landscape harm, particularly in certain viewpoints. However, this harm is
outweighed by the predicted ecological benefits, including the protection of
20

native species, restoration of coastal habitats, and enhancement of
biodiversity. The proposal aligns with key policies in the BIP, including NE1,
NE2, and NE3, which prioritise biodiversity and green infrastructure
improvements. While the fence introduces a man-made structure into a
protected landscape, its alignment and design minimise its visual impact,
and the project’s public benefits justify the limited harm.
106. I further assess that the planning conditions imposed, including a 15 year
time limit, a LEMP, and a Gate Access Management Plan, are necessary,
enforceable, and reasonable, with some minor adjustments recommended
for condition 2 regarding the fence’s finish. I am satisfied that the POA is an
appropriate vehicle to provide legal safeguards concerning maintenance and
decommissioning, although the Minister may wish to seek reassurances that
any land related contracts address future liabilities in full.
107. In conclusion, I assess the proposal to be in conformity with the BIP and I
recommend that the appeal should be DISMISSED. I further recommend
that, subject to a minor revision to condition 2, and to the terms of the POA,
the Minister should confirm the grant of planning permission as set out in
the Decision Notice. My suggested rewording for condition 2 is set out
below:
Condition 2: Precise details of the materials, finishes, and colours of the
fence posts, fencing mesh, capping and gates shall be submitted to and
approved in writing by the Chief Officer. Such details as are approved shall
thereafter be implemented in full and retained and maintained as such for
the duration of this permission.
P. Staddon
Mr Philip Staddon BSc, Dip, MBA, MRTPI
21

title: North Lynn Farm - Unit 1, agricultural shed and land, Field No. 764, La Rue du Clos Fallu, St. Martin: Planning Enforcement Notice: (ENF/2025/0002/0001A): Appeal Decision
text:
A decision made 20 February 2026:Decision Reference: MD-ENV-2026-158Public Subject: Planning enforcement appeal decision: ENF/2025/0002/0001a (Unit 1, Agricultural shed and land, North Lynn Farm, Field No. 764, La Rue du Clos Fallu, St. Martin) Report Title: Planning enforcement appeal decision: ENF/2025/0002/0001a (Unit 1, Agricultural shed and land, North Lynn Farm, Field No. 764, La Rue du Clos Fallu, St. Martin)Public Decision(s): The Assistant Minister decided to dismiss the appeal and that the enforcement notice should be upheld as corrected: (i) by replacing paragraph 3.1 by: “3.1 Without the necessary planning permission, the use of Unit 1 for the storage, repair and maintenance of vehicles not deployed in the joinery workshop business carried on in Unit 1.” and (ii) by replacing paragraph 5.1 by: “5.1 Cease the use of Unit 1 for the storage, repair and maintenance of vehicles not deployed in the joinery workshop business carried on in Unit 1.””.Reason for Decision(s):Having considered all aspects of the appeal the Assistant Minister decided to give effect to the inspector’s recommendations and was satisfied that there were no reasons not to do so.Resource Implications: There are no new financial and/or staffing implications arising as a consequence of this Ministerial Decision. Action Required: The Ministerial Office to advise the Judicial Greffe of the decision.Signature: Signed By: Constable M. Jackson of St. Brelade, Assistant MinisterDate Signed: Date of Decision (If different from Date Signed): 

 <h1

--- Inspector's Report ---

PLANNING AND BUILDING (JERSEY) LAW 2002
Appeal under Article 109 against an enforcement notice served under
Article 40(2)
REPORT TO THE MINISTER FOR THE ENVIRONMENT
made under Article 115(5)
by D A Hainsworth LL.B(Hons) FRSA Solicitor
the inspector nominated under Article 113(2) from the list of persons appointed
under Article 107
____________________________________________________________
Appellants:
North Lynn Farm Ltd
Enforcement notice reference number and date of issue:
ENF/2025/0002/0001a dated 17 June 2025
The land to which the enforcement notice relates:
Unit 1, Agricultural shed and land, North Lynn Farm, Field No. 764, La Rue du Clos
Fallu, St. Martin
The alleged breach of development controls:
“Without the necessary planning permission, changed the use of Unit 1 from
agricultural use to storage, repair and maintenance of vehicles (non-agricultural)”
The steps required by the enforcement notice:
“5.1 Cease the use of Unit 1 as vehicle storage, repair and maintenance, as well as
any other non-agricultural use.
5.2 Remove all resultant debris and waste from the land.”
Time for compliance with the notice:
Eight months
Grounds of appeal:
See Introduction below
Inspector’s site visit date:
15 December 2025
Hearing date:
16 December 2025
______________________________________________________

Inspector’s Report – Appeal by North Lynn Farm Ltd - Unit 1 – Ref. ENF/2025/0002/0001a
Introduction
1. The appeal has been made on grounds (a) (that the matters alleged in the
notice are not subject to control by this Law), (e) (that the matters alleged in
the notice have not in fact occurred) and (f) (that the requirements of or
conditions in the notice exceed what is reasonably necessary to remedy any
alleged breach of control or make good any injury to amenity). There has
been no appeal on ground (h) (that planning permission should be granted in
respect of the change of use): the planning merits of the use therefore do not
fall to be considered in the appeal and there is no planning application
associated with it.
2. On ground (a) it is claimed that the time allowed for taking enforcement
action pursuant to Article 40 has expired. This claim should have been made
on ground (c) (that at the date of service of the notice no or no expedient
action could be taken to remedy the alleged breach) and I have dealt with it
on that basis. On ground (e) it is maintained that the allegation has been
inaccurately described and on ground (f) it is claimed that the words “as well
as any other non-agricultural use” should not have been included in the steps
required by the notice.
Assessment
3. There has been considerable confusion about the authorised planning situation
of the building containing the units. I have attached an Appendix to this
report, which looks into this and concludes that Unit 1 has planning
permission for its use as a joinery workshop (P/2006/2743, granted on 9
February 2007 by the Minister for Planning & Environment, subject to the
conditions listed in paragraph 6 of the Appendix). This permission was
implemented and it supplanted permission P/2002/1553 as far as Unit 1 is
concerned.
4. The appellants state that the tenant has used the unit since 2013 as a small-
scale joinery workshop and builder’s store, with the incidental storage and
repair of the tenant’s own commercial and personal vehicles. A mobile joinery
workshop has also been created in a long wheelbase van, which is kept and
used inside the unit when it is not deployed off-site. This information is
supported by written statements, lease agreements, monthly bank statements
recording rental receipts, aerial photographs and oral evidence. The appellants
maintain that it is too late to take enforcement action because of the ‘8-year
rule’ in Article 40(1)(a) and they also question the expediency of taking
enforcement action, because the current tenant’s activities generate
significantly less disturbance than those of the tenant who occupied the unit
between 2007 and 2013 and used it to manufacture timber window frames.
5. The case presented by the Infrastructure & Environment Department relies
upon the information provided by the appellants, which they maintain points
to an unauthorised “hybrid” use, and upon paragraph 35 of the inspector’s
report referred to in paragraph 9 of the Appendix, which they maintain
records a material change of use from a joinery workshop to a vehicle storage
facility. Paragraph 35 reads as follows: -
“35. There is a further element of unauthorised use relating specifically to
unit 1. This is the unit where a permanent full permission was granted in 2007
for a joinery workshop (P/2006/2743). It does appear that such a use
2.

Inspector’s Report – Appeal by North Lynn Farm Ltd - Unit 1 – Ref. ENF/2025/0002/0001a
operated for some years, and I have noted residents submissions that
imposed planning conditions were routinely breached. However, when I visited
the unit, expecting to see an operational joinery workshop, I was surprised to
observe that there is a different use present. The unit contained a collection of
vehicles (cars and a van), a vehicle shell, an industrial style vehicle lift and
compressors. It is unclear whether this use is a commercial motor related
activity or a private car collection hobby type use, but it does appear to
constitute a material change of use from the previous (permitted) use as a
joinery workshop.”
6. The appellants are critical of paragraph 35 and the Department’s reliance on
it. The planning application the inspector was dealing with related to Units 2
and 6. The tenant of Unit 1 was not present at the time of the visit and an
explanation about what was observed there was not sought from him or the
appellants either at the site visit or at the subsequent hearing. The van
referred to is in fact the mobile joinery workshop, which was authorised to be
there, and the “industrial style vehicle lift” is a moveable vehicle storage
ramp. Paragraph 35 is, the appellants maintain, a comment seemingly made
in passing, about a situation said to be observed on one particular day that
cannot on its own demonstrate that a material change of use has occurred at
Unit 1.
7. When I inspected Unit 1 found it to be in commercial use as a joinery
workshop, with various items being manufactured there from wood and a
variety of tools and equipment being deployed for this purpose. The mobile
joinery workshop was in the unit and I saw that it was adapted and fully
equipped for joinery use both within the unit and off-site. Two cars and a
motorcycle belonging to the tenant were being stored in the unit that
appeared to have no connection with the joinery business. The vehicle storage
ramp was still there.
8. From what I have seen, read and heard in evidence about all these matters, it
appears to me on the balance of probability that what has taken place within
Unit 1 is as follows. The unit has been in continuous use as a joinery workshop
since planning permission was granted in 2007, but the extent of this use
within the unit has varied. The current occupier has used the unit to convert a
van into the mobile joinery workshop which is both stored there and used
there as a work station when it is not deployed elsewhere. He has also stored
other vehicles belonging to him there and worked on them there. He has in
addition from time to time allowed other people to store their vehicles in the
unit.
9. The significance of these matters for planning purposes, as a matter of fact
and degree, is as follows. The circumstances in which a planning permission
might in effect be held to be extinguished in law have not occurred; planning
permission P/2006/2743 is still extant; the use of the unit as a joinery
workshop is still authorised. The use of the unit by the mobile joinery
workshop is an incidental use that is functionally related to the authorised use
and does not materially affect its character; it is therefore authorised. The use
of the unit for the storage of vehicles not deployed in the joinery business,
whether belonging to the tenant or otherwise, and the carrying out of work on
them, is not incidental; it is an unauthorised use that is material because it
alters the character of the joinery workshop. Finally, there is insufficient
evidence that this unauthorised use has become immune from enforcement
3.

Inspector’s Report – Appeal by North Lynn Farm Ltd - Unit 1 – Ref. ENF/2025/0002/0001a
action under the ‘8-year rule’ or that it would not be expedient to take
enforcement action to remedy this breach.
10. As a result of these conclusions, the appeal on ground (c) should fail and the
appeals on grounds (e) and (f) should succeed to the extent that the
allegation in the notice and the steps required by the notice should be
corrected as set out in paragraph 11 below. The recommendations in
paragraph 11 will not prevent the continued use of Unit 1 as a joinery
workshop in accordance with the conditions in permission P/2006/2743.
Recommendations
11. The enforcement notice should be corrected: -
(i) by replacing paragraph 3.1 by:
“3.1 Without the necessary planning permission, the use of Unit 1 for the
storage, repair and maintenance of vehicles not deployed in the joinery
workshop business carried on in Unit 1.” and
(ii) by replacing paragraph 5.1 by:
“5.1 Cease the use of Unit 1 for the storage, repair and maintenance of
vehicles not deployed in the joinery workshop business carried on in Unit 1.”
12. Subject to these corrections, the appeal should be dismissed and the
enforcement notice should be upheld as corrected.
Dated 21 January 2026
D.A.Hainsworth
Inspector
Appendix attached as a separate document.
4.

North Lynn Farm Units 1, 2, 4, 5 & 6
Inspector’s enforcement appeals reports
Appendix to each of the reports
Background, planning history, commentary and conclusions
1. The power to serve an enforcement notice in respect of a breach of
development controls is in Article 40 of the Planning and Building (Jersey)
Law 2002. Two prerequisites apply, as follows, and both must be satisfied:
“(a) that there has been a breach of development controls during the previous 8
years; and
(b) that it is expedient that action should be taken to remedy the breach.”
Article 109(2) of the Law allows an appeal against an enforcement notice
to be made on the ground “(c) that at the date of service of the notice no or no
expedient action could be taken to remedy the alleged breach”.
Jersey law differs from the law in England and Wales in this respect since
in England and Wales there is no right of appeal against a planning
authority’s decision that it was “expedient” under section 172(1)(b) of the
Town and Country Planning Act 1990 to issue the enforcement notice: that
decision can only be challenged by way of judicial review in the courts.
2. The construction of the building containing the units was authorised by
two planning permissions granted in 1989 and 1995, both of which were
implemented. These are D/1989/0921 and D/1992/0003. Permission
D/1989/0921 relates to an L-shaped building described as an “agricultural
shed”; it now contains Units 1, 2, 3 & 5. Permission D/1992/0003 relates
to an extension to “existing agricultural shed”, making the building
rectangular, as it exists today; it now contains Units 4 & 6. Neither of the
permissions contain any ongoing planning conditions relevant to the
appeals nor any restrictions on the manner of use of the building, other
than its description as an “agricultural shed”.
3. These permissions were followed by planning permission P/2002/1553,
which was granted by the Environment and Public Services Committee on
14 August 2003. The permission was implemented. It authorised the
change of use of the building (then described as “cattle shed and potato
store”) to “dry storage”. The permission was subject to nine conditions, as
follows: -
“1. The use hereby permitted shall cease on or before 22nd August 2006
and the land and building restored to its former agricultural use.
2. Notwithstanding the provisions of the Island Planning (Use
Classes)(Jersey) Regulations, 1965 and the Island Planning (Exempted
Development)(Jersey) Regulations, 1965, or any subsequent
1

amendment thereto, the shed at ‘North Lynn Farm, La Rue du Clos
Fallu, St, Martin’, shall be used for dry storage and for no retailing,
industrial business or manufacturing use.
3. That details of the prospective occupants of the premises shall be
submitted to and approved in writing by the Environment and Public
Services Committee prior to the occupation of the building. This
includes includes [sic] any subsequent changes in occupancy in
perpetuity.
4. The building shall not be subdivided or sublet to form smaller
individual units without the prior written consent of the Environment
and Public Services Committee
5. No outside storage or display of materials, waste, machinery or
vehicles shall take place on the site, unless otherwise agreed in writing
with the Environment and Public Services Committee
6. That any external alterations or sinage [sic] shall need to be made
the subject of a seperate [sic] application.
7. The development hereby permitted shall not be commenced until
there has been submitted to and approved in writing by the
Environment and Public Services Committee, a scheme of landscaping
which shall provide details of planting around the new access to be
created on to La Rue du Clos Fallu, including any excavation works,
surfacing treatments, or means of enclosure and confirmation that all
existing agricultural machinery and other related items (currently
stored outside the shed) shall be removed from the site and the site left
clear before the new use is commenced.
8. No user shall be agreed who requires to use P30 vehicles to access
this site as the site access and turning area is inadequate for such large
commercial vehicles.
9. The use hereby permitted shall only operate between the hours of
8am and 6pm, Monday to Friday and 8am and 1pm on Saturdays and
not at all on Sundays and Bank Holidays.”
4. The next relevant event in the building’s planning history is the meeting of
the Environment and Public Services Committee on 30 September 2005.
The Committee considered a request to reconsider the conditions attached
to planning permission P/2002/1553. Extracts from the Committee
Minutes read as follows: -
“The Committee recalled that the current permission for dry storage use
had expired on 14th August 2005 [This was an error. The permission was
due to expire on 22 August 2006], and the applicant had indicated a wish
to sub-divide the shed for a number of smaller users, which would not be
permissible under the conditions applied to the original approval
(aforementioned Minute refers).
2

The Committee noted that the current plans contained a small joinery
workshop which was not considered acceptable under the existing dry
storage permit, and as such the Committee directed officer[s] to inform
the applicant that this should be deleted from the scheme.
The Committee was informed that part of the shed was still used by the
owners for agricultural related storage, and that the sub-division of the
shed into smaller units did not necessarily pose a problem as the site had
acceptable vehicular access and sufficient surrounding land to enable
several users to operate on the site. The Committee was of the opinion,
therefore, that once a small area was retained by the owners and the
proposed joinery workshop had been removed from the scheme, the shed
could be divided into two separate areas without compromising the
operation of the site.
The Committee therefore approved renewal of the permission of
the existing dry storage permit for a further three years [i.e.
presumably until 22 August 2009, or it could be three years from the date
of the Committee’s meeting], and approved the division of the shed
into two separate areas. The Committee noted, however, that all
existing conditions applied to this site remained, and that this
would preclude any further sub-division.”
5. Building application R/2006/0556 was then submitted on 23 March 2006
to “Subdivide existing storage building to create part industrial premise”.
This application related to what is now Unit 1. The official register
indicates that the drawings/plans were approved.
6. Planning permission P/2006/2743 was then granted on 9 February 2007
by the Minister for Planning & Environment. It authorised the “Change of
use of part of store into joinery workshop”. This part is also now Unit 1.
The permission was implemented. It supplanted permission P/2002/1553
as respects Unit 1, which thereafter no longer applied to this part of the
building. Conditions were imposed in P/2006/2743 as follows: -
“1. Any externally audible plant or equipment shall comply with Noise
Rating Curve NR45 measured 1 metre from the boundary of the nearest
affected property.
2. No outside storage or display of materials, waste, machinery, or
vehicles, shall take place on the site unless otherwise agreed in writing
with the Minister for Planning and Environment.
3. The hours of operation of the joinery workshop shall be limited to
08.00am to 06.00pm Monday - Saturday, and no working on Sundays or
Public Holidays.”
7. The next relevant event in the building’s planning history is Building
Permit B/2010/0666, which was issued on 29 July 2010 for works
described as “Construct partition in dry storage shed”. This authorised the
formation of Unit 5 within the building in respect of the Building Bye-laws.
The works were carried out later in 2010.
3

8. No action to enforce Condition 1 (the time limit) of planning permission
P/2002/1253 was taken before the expiry (presumably in 2017 at the
latest) of the time limit in Article 40 for taking enforcement action
requiring the use of the building as dry storage to cease and the building
to be restored to its former agricultural use.
9. In 2023, planning application P/2023/1291 was submitted for the “Change
of use of Unit 2 from dry storage to mechanical repair workshop and Unit
6 from dry store to manufacture of garden pots, reposition vehicle access,
and retrospective removal of conditions 1, 2, and 4 as well as variation of
conditions 3 and 5 under planning permit P/2002/1553”. The application
was refused and an appeal against the decision was dismissed for the
legal reasons explained in the inspector’s report (see paragraphs 13 and
14 below). The report resulted in the paradoxical situation that breaches
of planning conditions were found to have occurred in circumstances
where the planning permission relating to them was found not to have
been implemented lawfully and the planning conditions themselves were
found not to be operative.
10.The enforcement notices now served relate to Units 1, 2, 4, 5 & 6. The
plans attached to the notices draw a red line only around the units
themselves within the building. The notices do not relate to any land
adjoining any unit or any of the external areas around the building as a
whole. My reports are therefore confined solely to the activities taking
place within the individual units themselves.
11.Except in relation to Unit 1 (which as recorded in paragraph 6 above has
its own freestanding planning permission) and Unit 3 (which has remained
in agricultural use throughout), the situation that now arises on the
enforcement appeals is as follows: -
(a) if, as the previous inspector concluded, planning permission
P/2002/1553 was not lawfully implemented and even if had it been
the conditions attached to it would nevertheless no longer be
operative, and
(b) if any of the current enforcement appeals succeed on the basis that
it is now too late to take enforcement action against unauthorised
uses taking place there at present, then
(c) the outcome would be that the enforcement notices relating to
these units would have to be quashed and, as a result,
(d) the unauthorised uses in these units could then continue
unconstrained by any of the conditions imposed on planning
permission P/2002/1553 and there would be no power to impose
new or alternative replacement conditions.
12.This is not an outcome that appears to have been foreseen by the
Infrastructure & Environment Department. Up to the time of the previous
inspector’s report, all concerned had treated the conditions imposed by
P/2002/1553 as being valid and ongoing. I have therefore re-assessed the
legal reasons previously arrived at, which were based on the cases of
Whitley & Sons v Secretary of State for Wales (1992) 64 P&CR 296 and
Avon Estates Ltd v Welsh Ministers & Ceredigion County Council [2011]
4

EWCA Civ 553. I have reached different conclusions to the previous
inspector for the reasons set out in paragraphs 15 to 24 below.
13. As to Whitley, the inspector stated in his report:
“29. A legal issue here is that a number of the planning conditions
attached to P/2002/1553 were what are known as ‘conditions precedent’.
This means that they have to be complied with before the permitted use
(dry storage in this case) can commence. Notably, the submission and
approval of occupiers did not appear to take place, nor does there seem to
be any record of the landscaping scheme requirements being submitted
and approved, which was again a pre-commencement requirement. At the
Hearing, the appellant’s agent confirmed that he believed that his client
did not seek approval of the occupiers (as required by condition 3) and he
‘can’t be sure’ that landscaping details were submitted pursuant to
condition 7.
30. UK case law, known as the ‘Whitley Principle’ dating back to a case in
1992, and confirmed in later judgments, has established that breaching a
condition that goes to the heart of a permission, renders its
implementation unlawful. Having examined the phrasing and effect of the
conditions precedent in this case, I am not convinced that the landscape
scheme condition crosses that threshold, but I do consider that the
occupancy condition does. I therefore have to conclude that P/2002/1553
was not lawfully implemented.”
14. As to Avon Estates, the inspector stated in his report, at paragraph 32:
“32. Even if the Minister did not agree with my assessment above, there is
a further legal principle that results in a fatal flaw to application
P/2023/1291, which is the subject of this appeal. This relates to the
inability to vary or remove conditions from a temporary permit, once the
temporary permitted use period has expired. The end date of the
permission was 22 August 2006. Whilst it may have become a moving
feast, as a result of the curious September 2005 committee decision, at
its latest it would extend to 22 August 2009, a date that has long since
passed.”
and at paragraph 34:
“34. In the absence of any known Jersey legal cases on these matters, I
have again reverted to UK planning case law. This establishes that what is
being proposed by the application simply cannot be entertained. The key
case here is Avon Estates Ltd v Welsh Ministers [2011] EWCA Civ 553.
This established that, following the expiry of the time limited condition,
the permission no longer authorised the development (in this case the dry
storage use), and the conditions attached to it could no longer bind the
land or be enforced. It follows that such conditions cannot therefore be
the subject of proposals to remove or vary them.”
15. However, later court decisions have confirmed that there are exceptions
to Whitley and that the categories of the exceptions are not closed. One
5

exception is that a later validation can be obtained. The courts have also
decided that the Whitley principle should be applied flexibly. It has also
been decided in one case (R (Hart Aggregates Ltd) v Hartlepool Borough
Council [2005] EWHC 840 (Admin)) that it would be both irrational and an
abuse of power for enforcement action to be commenced on the basis that
a permission had not been lawfully implemented some 34 years earlier.
16. P/2002/1553 was granted on 14 August 2003. I have no information as
to when the building was first occupied for dry storage (by which time
Condition 3 relating to occupancy should have been complied with), but
the details of the occupants would have been available to the Committee
when they met on 30 September 2005. The Committee however raised no
concerns about the matter even though the occupancy of the building was
under discussion. It is reasonable to infer that this constituted ‘later
validation’, but even if this were not the case it is clearly unreasonable,
applying Hart, to take enforcement action now on the basis that Condition
3 was not complied with over 20 years ago and the permission granted in
2003 was therefore not lawfully implemented. Article 40 would not be
complied with because it would not be “expedient that action should be
taken”.
17. Avon Estates was a decision relating to the provisions of English and
Welsh planning law concerning seasonal occupancy conditions in certain
planning permissions granted for a temporary period at a holiday
accommodation site near the Welsh coast. I do not agree with the
conclusions set out in paragraphs 32 & 34 of the inspector’s report.
18.In the report, the words “and after the time limit for taking enforcement
action has expired” should be added after “expired” in paragraph 32 and
again after the words “following the expiry of the time limited condition” in
the analysis of Avon Estates in paragraph 34. If it were otherwise, the
ongoing planning conditions in P/2002/1553 could not have been enforced
in the period between the expiry of the time limit on the permission and
the expiry of the time limit for taking enforcement action for non-
compliance with the time limit. These omissions did not affect the
outcome of the report since the time limit for taking enforcement action
had expired in 2017 at the latest in this instance.
19. English and Welsh planning law makes specific provision for the
imposition of conditions on planning permissions “for requiring the
removal of any buildings or works authorised by the permission, or the
discontinuance of any use of land so authorised, at the end of a specified
period, and the carrying out of any works required for the reinstatement
of land at the end of that period”. Such a planning permission is referred
to in the law as ‘planning permission granted for a limited period’. (See
Town and Country Planning Act 1990, sections 72(1)(b) & 72(2).)
20.In the Avon Estates decision, Sir David Keene gave an obiter dictum
opinion at paragraph 28, as follows: “One cannot derive a general
approval … for conditions which bind the land once the development itself
has ceased to be authorised and has become immune from enforcement
action. … So although I would not wish to be categoric as to the
6

impossibility of such enduring conditions, I do regard it as very unlikely
that the statutory scheme allows for what can be described as a
permanent condition on a temporary permission, other than the time limit
condition itself.”. This opinion is not a sound enough basis on which to
reach the unequivocal conclusions referencing Avon Estates that are in
paragraph 34 of the inspector’s report.
21. There are no equivalent provisions in Jersey planning law comparable to
the statutory scheme in England and Wales. In fact, Jersey planning law
contains no provisions at all specifically relating to temporary planning
permissions or to planning permissions granted for a limited period.
(Article 26 only relates to conditions prescribing the time limit for the
commencement of development and the service of notices requiring its
completion once it has begun.) The interpretation of Jersey law is a matter
for the Jersey courts to determine, but in my opinion in the absence of a
statutory scheme in Jersey the courts could make decisions unconstrained
by Sir David Keene’s obiter dictum and, even if they did apply it, they
would be able to define the extent of what was allowable in Jersey and to
apply their own conclusions in the specific circumstances relating to the
enforcement of the ongoing conditions imposed on planning permission
P/2002/1553, taking expediency into account.
22. In Avon Estates, the Welsh planning authority’s opinion that the
conditions still applied in the circumstances of that case was challenged by
the developer. In the present case, up to the time of the previous
inspector’s report on 28 November 2024, all concerned in Jersey had
treated the building (except Units 1 and 3) on the basis that it was subject
to the ongoing conditions imposed by P/2002/1553.
23.Since all concerned have for so very many years treated the permission
and its ongoing conditions as still being in force and have always
conducted their dealings on that basis, it would not be expedient now to
treat the building (except for Units 1 and 3) on any other basis. Support
for this approach exists in a line of England and Wales court decisions
beginning with R v Caradon DC ex parte Knott [2000] 3 PLR 1 in which
the court held that since the parties had conducted their dealings on the
basis that a certain planning permission existed, it would be unjust now
for the planning authority to proceed otherwise: the principle of estoppel
by convention applied.
24.For the above reasons, I have therefore concluded that planning
permission P/2002/1553 is still subsisting in respect of Units 2, 4, 5 and 6
and that the use of these units is subject to the ongoing conditions of the
permission, namely Conditions 2, 3 (subsequent changes in occupancy),
4, 5, 6, 8 and 9.
Dated 21 January 2026
D.A.Hainsworth
Inspector
7

title: North Lynn Farm - Unit 2, agricultural shed and land, Field No. 764, La Rue du Clos Fallu, St. Martin: Planning Enforcement Notice: (ENF/2025/0002/0002A): Appeal Decision
text:
--- Inspector's Report ---

PLANNING AND BUILDING (JERSEY) LAW 2002
Appeal under Article 109 against an enforcement notice served under
Article 40(2)
REPORT TO THE MINISTER FOR THE ENVIRONMENT
made under Article 115(5)
by D A Hainsworth LL.B(Hons) FRSA Solicitor
the inspector nominated under Article 113(2) from the list of persons appointed
under Article 107
____________________________________________________________
Appellants:
North Lynn Farm Ltd
Enforcement notice reference number and date of issue:
ENF/2025/0002/0002a dated 17 June 2025
The land to which the enforcement notice relates:
Unit 2, Agricultural shed and land, North Lynn Farm, Field No. 764, La Rue du Clos
Fallu, St. Martin.
The alleged breach of development controls:
“Without the necessary planning permission, the change of use of Unit 2 from
agricultural use to vehicle storage, repair and maintenance (non-agricultural)”
The steps required by the enforcement notice:
“5.1 Cease the use of Unit 2 for vehicle storage, repair and maintenance, as well as
any other non-agricultural use.
5.2 Remove all resultant debris and waste from the land.”
Time for compliance with the notice:
Eight months
Ground of appeal:
Ground (f): “that the requirements of or conditions in the notice exceed what is
reasonably necessary to remedy any alleged breach of control or make good any
injury to amenity”.
Inspector’s site visit date:
15 December 2025
Hearing date:
16 December 2025

Inspector’s Report – Appeal by North Lynn Farm Ltd – Unit 2 - Ref. ENF/2025/0002/0002a
Introduction
1. The appellants have acknowledged that a change of use has occurred in
breach of development controls. The tenant of Unit 2 intends to cease the
unauthorised use within the eight months’ compliance period. The appeal has
been brought solely on the basis that the words “as well as any other non-
agricultural use” should not have been included in the requirement in
paragraph 5.1 of the notice.
2. There has been no appeal on ground (h) (that planning permission should be
granted in respect of the change of use). The planning merits of the use
therefore do not fall to be considered in the appeal and there is no planning
application associated with it.
Assessment
3. There has been considerable confusion about the authorised planning situation
of the building containing the units. I have attached an Appendix to this
report, which looks into this and reaches the conclusion that planning
permission P/2002/1553 is still subsisting in respect of Units 2, 4, 5 and 6 and
that the use of these units is subject to the ongoing conditions of the
permission, namely Conditions 2, 3 (subsequent changes in occupancy), 4, 5,
6, 8 and 9.
4. As a result of this conclusion, the allegation in the enforcement notice should
be corrected by replacing “agricultural” by “dry storage”. This does not affect
the appellants’ acknowledgement that there has been a breach of
development controls, since vehicle repair and maintenance are not dry
storage but are an industrial business which is expressly prohibited by
Condition 2 of permission P/2002/1553. The steps required by the notice
should also be corrected by deleting the word “storage”, because vehicle
storage is a form of dry storage that is permitted.
5. I turn now to the reason given for the appeal, as explained in paragraph 2
above. The steps that can be required by an enforcement notice are specified
in Article 40(3)(b) of the Law and are limited to those required to remedy the
breach or make good any injury to amenity caused by the breach. The notice
cannot require a lawful use to be resumed and the steps required by the
notice must be consistent with the allegations in the notice. The breach of
control and the injury to amenity would be remedied in this instance simply by
ceasing the use of the unit for vehicle repair and maintenance. The additional
requirement to cease “any other non-agricultural use” exceeds what is
necessary to remedy the breach or make good any injury to amenity caused
by it.
Recommendations
6. The appeal should succeed to the extent that the enforcement notice should
be corrected by: -
(i) replacing “agricultural” by “dry storage” in paragraph 3.1,
(ii) deleting “storage,” in paragraph 5.1 and
(iii) deleting “, as well as any other non-agricultural use” in paragraph 5.1.
2.

Inspector’s Report – Appeal by North Lynn Farm Ltd – Unit 2 - Ref. ENF/2025/0002/0002a
7. The appeal should be dismissed in all other respects and the enforcement
notice should be upheld as corrected.
Dated 21 January 2026
D.A.Hainsworth
Inspector
Appendix attached as a separate document.
3.

North Lynn Farm Units 1, 2, 4, 5 & 6
Inspector’s enforcement appeals reports
Appendix to each of the reports
Background, planning history, commentary and conclusions
1. The power to serve an enforcement notice in respect of a breach of
development controls is in Article 40 of the Planning and Building (Jersey)
Law 2002. Two prerequisites apply, as follows, and both must be satisfied:
“(a) that there has been a breach of development controls during the previous 8
years; and
(b) that it is expedient that action should be taken to remedy the breach.”
Article 109(2) of the Law allows an appeal against an enforcement notice
to be made on the ground “(c) that at the date of service of the notice no or no
expedient action could be taken to remedy the alleged breach”.
Jersey law differs from the law in England and Wales in this respect since
in England and Wales there is no right of appeal against a planning
authority’s decision that it was “expedient” under section 172(1)(b) of the
Town and Country Planning Act 1990 to issue the enforcement notice: that
decision can only be challenged by way of judicial review in the courts.
2. The construction of the building containing the units was authorised by
two planning permissions granted in 1989 and 1995, both of which were
implemented. These are D/1989/0921 and D/1992/0003. Permission
D/1989/0921 relates to an L-shaped building described as an “agricultural
shed”; it now contains Units 1, 2, 3 & 5. Permission D/1992/0003 relates
to an extension to “existing agricultural shed”, making the building
rectangular, as it exists today; it now contains Units 4 & 6. Neither of the
permissions contain any ongoing planning conditions relevant to the
appeals nor any restrictions on the manner of use of the building, other
than its description as an “agricultural shed”.
3. These permissions were followed by planning permission P/2002/1553,
which was granted by the Environment and Public Services Committee on
14 August 2003. The permission was implemented. It authorised the
change of use of the building (then described as “cattle shed and potato
store”) to “dry storage”. The permission was subject to nine conditions, as
follows: -
“1. The use hereby permitted shall cease on or before 22nd August 2006
and the land and building restored to its former agricultural use.
2. Notwithstanding the provisions of the Island Planning (Use
Classes)(Jersey) Regulations, 1965 and the Island Planning (Exempted
Development)(Jersey) Regulations, 1965, or any subsequent
1

amendment thereto, the shed at ‘North Lynn Farm, La Rue du Clos
Fallu, St, Martin’, shall be used for dry storage and for no retailing,
industrial business or manufacturing use.
3. That details of the prospective occupants of the premises shall be
submitted to and approved in writing by the Environment and Public
Services Committee prior to the occupation of the building. This
includes includes [sic] any subsequent changes in occupancy in
perpetuity.
4. The building shall not be subdivided or sublet to form smaller
individual units without the prior written consent of the Environment
and Public Services Committee
5. No outside storage or display of materials, waste, machinery or
vehicles shall take place on the site, unless otherwise agreed in writing
with the Environment and Public Services Committee
6. That any external alterations or sinage [sic] shall need to be made
the subject of a seperate [sic] application.
7. The development hereby permitted shall not be commenced until
there has been submitted to and approved in writing by the
Environment and Public Services Committee, a scheme of landscaping
which shall provide details of planting around the new access to be
created on to La Rue du Clos Fallu, including any excavation works,
surfacing treatments, or means of enclosure and confirmation that all
existing agricultural machinery and other related items (currently
stored outside the shed) shall be removed from the site and the site left
clear before the new use is commenced.
8. No user shall be agreed who requires to use P30 vehicles to access
this site as the site access and turning area is inadequate for such large
commercial vehicles.
9. The use hereby permitted shall only operate between the hours of
8am and 6pm, Monday to Friday and 8am and 1pm on Saturdays and
not at all on Sundays and Bank Holidays.”
4. The next relevant event in the building’s planning history is the meeting of
the Environment and Public Services Committee on 30 September 2005.
The Committee considered a request to reconsider the conditions attached
to planning permission P/2002/1553. Extracts from the Committee
Minutes read as follows: -
“The Committee recalled that the current permission for dry storage use
had expired on 14th August 2005 [This was an error. The permission was
due to expire on 22 August 2006], and the applicant had indicated a wish
to sub-divide the shed for a number of smaller users, which would not be
permissible under the conditions applied to the original approval
(aforementioned Minute refers).
2

The Committee noted that the current plans contained a small joinery
workshop which was not considered acceptable under the existing dry
storage permit, and as such the Committee directed officer[s] to inform
the applicant that this should be deleted from the scheme.
The Committee was informed that part of the shed was still used by the
owners for agricultural related storage, and that the sub-division of the
shed into smaller units did not necessarily pose a problem as the site had
acceptable vehicular access and sufficient surrounding land to enable
several users to operate on the site. The Committee was of the opinion,
therefore, that once a small area was retained by the owners and the
proposed joinery workshop had been removed from the scheme, the shed
could be divided into two separate areas without compromising the
operation of the site.
The Committee therefore approved renewal of the permission of
the existing dry storage permit for a further three years [i.e.
presumably until 22 August 2009, or it could be three years from the date
of the Committee’s meeting], and approved the division of the shed
into two separate areas. The Committee noted, however, that all
existing conditions applied to this site remained, and that this
would preclude any further sub-division.”
5. Building application R/2006/0556 was then submitted on 23 March 2006
to “Subdivide existing storage building to create part industrial premise”.
This application related to what is now Unit 1. The official register
indicates that the drawings/plans were approved.
6. Planning permission P/2006/2743 was then granted on 9 February 2007
by the Minister for Planning & Environment. It authorised the “Change of
use of part of store into joinery workshop”. This part is also now Unit 1.
The permission was implemented. It supplanted permission P/2002/1553
as respects Unit 1, which thereafter no longer applied to this part of the
building. Conditions were imposed in P/2006/2743 as follows: -
“1. Any externally audible plant or equipment shall comply with Noise
Rating Curve NR45 measured 1 metre from the boundary of the nearest
affected property.
2. No outside storage or display of materials, waste, machinery, or
vehicles, shall take place on the site unless otherwise agreed in writing
with the Minister for Planning and Environment.
3. The hours of operation of the joinery workshop shall be limited to
08.00am to 06.00pm Monday - Saturday, and no working on Sundays or
Public Holidays.”
7. The next relevant event in the building’s planning history is Building
Permit B/2010/0666, which was issued on 29 July 2010 for works
described as “Construct partition in dry storage shed”. This authorised the
formation of Unit 5 within the building in respect of the Building Bye-laws.
The works were carried out later in 2010.
3

8. No action to enforce Condition 1 (the time limit) of planning permission
P/2002/1253 was taken before the expiry (presumably in 2017 at the
latest) of the time limit in Article 40 for taking enforcement action
requiring the use of the building as dry storage to cease and the building
to be restored to its former agricultural use.
9. In 2023, planning application P/2023/1291 was submitted for the “Change
of use of Unit 2 from dry storage to mechanical repair workshop and Unit
6 from dry store to manufacture of garden pots, reposition vehicle access,
and retrospective removal of conditions 1, 2, and 4 as well as variation of
conditions 3 and 5 under planning permit P/2002/1553”. The application
was refused and an appeal against the decision was dismissed for the
legal reasons explained in the inspector’s report (see paragraphs 13 and

of planning conditions were found to have occurred in circumstances
where the planning permission relating to them was found not to have
been implemented lawfully and the planning conditions themselves were
found not to be operative.
10.The enforcement notices now served relate to Units 1, 2, 4, 5 & 6. The
plans attached to the notices draw a red line only around the units
themselves within the building. The notices do not relate to any land
adjoining any unit or any of the external areas around the building as a
whole. My reports are therefore confined solely to the activities taking
place within the individual units themselves.
11.Except in relation to Unit 1 (which as recorded in paragraph 6 above has
its own freestanding planning permission) and Unit 3 (which has remained
in agricultural use throughout), the situation that now arises on the
enforcement appeals is as follows: -
(a) if, as the previous inspector concluded, planning permission
P/2002/1553 was not lawfully implemented and even if had it been
the conditions attached to it would nevertheless no longer be
operative, and
(b) if any of the current enforcement appeals succeed on the basis that
it is now too late to take enforcement action against unauthorised
uses taking place there at present, then
(c) the outcome would be that the enforcement notices relating to
these units would have to be quashed and, as a result,

condition 7.

was not lawfully implemented.”

to when the building was first occupied for dry storage (by which time

17. Avon Estates was a decision relating to the provisions of English and

accommodation site near the Welsh coast. I do not agree with the
conclusions set out in paragraphs 32 & 34 of the inspector’s report.
18.In the report, the words “and after the time limit for taking enforcement
action has expired” should be added after “expired” in paragraph 32 and
again after the words “following the expiry of the time limited condition” in
the analysis of Avon Estates in paragraph 34. If it were otherwise, the

had expired in 2017 at the latest in this instance.
19. English and Welsh planning law makes specific provision for the
imposition of conditions on planning permissions “for requiring the

period, and the carrying out of any works required for the reinstatement

6

the developer. In the present case, up to the time of the previous
inspector’s report on 28 November 2024, all concerned in Jersey had
treated the building (except Units 1 and 3) on the basis that it was subject
to the ongoing conditions imposed by P/2002/1553.
