 <h1

--- Inspector's Report ---

PLANNING AND BUILDING (JERSEY) LAW 2002 (AS AMENDED)
Appeal under Article 109 (1) (d) of an enforcement notice served under Article 40
Report to the Minister
By Sue Bell MSc., BSc, FCIEEM, CEcol, CWEM,
An Inspector appointed under Article 107
Appellant: Ms Nadia Miller
Enforcement Notice reference number: ENF/2025/00016/001
Date of enforcement notice: 7 August 2025
Location: Restaurant (formerly Nude Dunes), Route de la Pulente, St Brelade,
JE3 8HG.
Matters which appear to constitute the breach of development controls: without
the necessary planning permission, creation of a self-contained residential unit.
Appeal procedure and date: site inspection and hearing (conjoined with appeal
against Enforcement Notice ENF/2025/00016/002). Both held 12 November 2025.
Date of report: 8 December 2025
Preliminary matters
1. The appellant has received and appealed two Enforcement Notices for the
same property. I conjoined the site inspections and hearings for these
appeals. This report deals only with the appeal against Enforcement Notice
ENF/2025/00016/001. My recommendations in respect of the appeal against
Enforcement Notice ENF/2025/00016/002 are reported separately.
2. Article 109 (2) of the Planning and Building (Jersey) Law 2002 (as amended)
(‘the Law’) specifies the permissible grounds for appeal against an
Enforcement Notice. The appellant’s appeal form identified a single ground
of appeal, Ground (e) – the matters alleged in the notice have in fact not
occurred. However, in the supporting Statement of Case, the appellant
suggested that the Enforcement Notice had not been served on the owner of
the land, in line with Article 40(2) of the Law. I interpreted these comments
as potentially raising an appeal under Ground (d) - that the person was not
the proper person to be served with such a Notice. Following my request for
further information, the appellant has confirmed that she wishes to appeal
the Notice under both Grounds (d) and (e). The Department has had the
opportunity to comment on this additional ground of appeal.
1

Introduction
3. This appeal against the Enforcement Notice was made under the following
grounds as provided for by section 109 (2) of the Planning and Building
(Jersey) Law 2002:
(d) that the person was not the proper person to be served with such
a notice; and
(e) that the matters alleged in the notice have not in fact occurred.
4. The appeal site has an extensive planning history, which is summarised in the
appellant’s Statement of Case and is not repeated here. There is no dispute
between parties that the authorised use of the property is a café, consented
through permission P/2015/1600. Its most recent use as a restaurant is within
the same use class (Class B) of the Planning and Building (General
Development) (Jersey) Order 2011 (‘the GDO’) and hence is deemed to have
planning permission. Whilst the company operating the restaurant entered
bankruptcy on 30th November 2023, there has been no change in the
authorised use of the building. Applications to change the use of the building
have been submitted, but at the time of writing none of these has been
successful.
Ground (d) that the person was not the proper person to be served with such a
notice.
5. The appellant considers that the requirements of Article 40 (2) of the Law
have not been met as the Enforcement Notice was not served on the owner
of the land. The appellant’s Statement of Case (paragraph 1.7) comments
that the Notice was served on Ms Nadia Miller at Restaurant (formerly Nude
Dunes) and on ‘The Occupant, Restaurant (formerly Nude Dunes) Route de la
Pulente’. It asserts that Ms Miller is not the owner of the property, which is
owned by Nude Food Dunes (2020) Ltd, that the Restaurant is not Ms Miller’s
address and that there is no occupant of the property.
6. In response to my request for further information, the Department confirmed
that separate notices were served on Ms Nadia Miller and ‘The Occupier’ at
the Restaurant and a separate notice was sent to ‘The Directors’ of the
Company, at Malorey Manor, which is the Registered Office address for Nude
Food Dunes (2020) Ltd.
7. Based on discussions at the hearing, I understand that the Department hand
delivered two paper copies of the Notice to the appeal property. One of these
was addressed directly to Ms Miller and the other was addressed to ‘The
Occupier’. As the property has no post box, copies were left by the door. A
third paper copy of the notice was sent via Jersey Post to the registered
offices of Nude Food Dunes (2020) Ltd. Separately, an electronic copy of the
Notice addressed to Ms Miller was emailed to her. Ms Miller confirmed that
she received the email copy. She noted that one of the paper copies left at
the door of the appeal property was found by a member of the public and
2

passed to her, but the other one was never received. There is no confirmation
that the paper copy sent to the registered address for Nude Food Dunes (2020)
Ltd was received.
8. Article 40 (2) states:
“Where this Article applies the Chief Officer or, as the case may be, the
Planning Committee may serve an enforcement notice on –
(a) the owner of the land;
(b) the occupier of the land (if different); and
(c) any other person who appears to be causing or responsible for the
breach.”
9. The Department has argued that this allows discretion as to who the Notice
may be served on. I do not align with this view. It seems to me that any
discretion is only in relation to whether to serve a Notice. Where a Notice is
served, it should be served on those listed under items (a) to (c) of
Article 40 (2).
10. The appellant has clarified that the appeal property is owned by Nude Food
Dunes (2020) Ltd, which has a single Director, who is also company secretary.
The appellant is the sole shareholder. Decisions about day-to-day
management of the property are made by the appellant, in consultation/
approval with the Director. The Notice was served on both the appellant, who
is the sole shareholder and who makes decisions about the use of the property
and the Director of the company at the company’s registered office address.
I therefore conclude that the Notice was served on the appropriate people,
as representatives of the company that owns the property. A copy was also
served on the occupier at the appeal property. I am satisfied that there were
no other people who should have been served with the notice.
11. Whilst the Department has proof of postage to the registered office of the
company, it was unable to provide proof of delivery or receipt of any of the
letters – either in the form of recorded/ signed for delivery of the paper
notices and/or photographs of leaving the paper copies at the appeal site.
Given the legal status and significance of these documents, I would expect
proof of receipt or at least delivery to be available.
12. Notwithstanding my comments above, I do not consider that any one has been
prejudiced in terms of being unaware of the Notice. Both the sole Director of
the company and the sole shareholder were aware of the terms of the notice.
Indeed, although the Director has not appealed the notice on behalf of the
company, he was present at the hearing.
13. For the above reasons, I recommend that the appeal under Ground (d) fails.
Ground (e) that the matters alleged in the notice have not in fact occurred.
14. There is no dispute that the authorised use of the property is a café,
consented through permission P/2015/1600. Its most recent use as a
3

restaurant is within the same use class (Class B) of the Planning and Building
(General Development) (Jersey) Order 2011 (‘the GDO’) and hence is deemed
to have planning permission. Whilst the company operating the restaurant
entered bankruptcy on 30th November 2023, there has been no change in the
authorised use of the building. Applications to change the use of the building
have been submitted, but at the time of the hearing none of these had been
granted.
15. Determining whether a change in use has occurred is a matter of fact and
degree. In Scotland and England, the burden of proof in appeals against
Enforcement Notices lies with the appellant. In the absence of guidance to
the contrary, I have taken this principle to apply in Jersey.
16. The appellant has directed me to definitions of a dwelling-house and dwelling
as provided in the GDO and the Bridging Island Plan 2022 respectively and
concluded that a self-contained residential unit is not a category of
development recognised in law. I do not find these definitions particularly
helpful. Not all planning uses are defined within the GDO. Residential use is
sui generis and a change in use from a café/restaurant (Class B) to residential
use (sui generis) would require planning permission.
17. The term ‘residential’ is typically understood to refer to the places where
people live. A property need not be occupied every night or on a regular basis
for it to be considered in residential use. Nor does it need to be the sole
abode of the occupier(s). In addition, a property need not have been designed
as a dwelling to be in residential use. The determining factor is whether the
use of that building is residential in character. Residential use may also take
place alongside other uses.
18. The Department commenced its investigation of potential residential use of
the property following a site visit it conducted on 10th June 2025, when a
person, accompanied by three children, was observed to leave the property
carrying a laundry basket. A follow-up visit was made, at the invitation of the
appellant. The presence of beds and other domestic items on that visit,
combined with the presence of similar items during the planning committee
visit led to the issuing of the Enforcement Notice.
19. The appellant does not dispute that the property has been occupied on some
nights, either by the appellant and her family or by an acquaintance. The
appellant states that these stays have been undertaken for security reasons,
to indicate that the site has not been abandoned. She also acknowledges that
beds and other domestic items were in place during site inspections by the
planning committee and planning inspector in connection with previous
planning applications and appeals at the site. These were placed to ‘dress’
the property to provide an illustration of how it might look if permission were
granted to convert it to holiday accommodation.
20. The appellant maintains that there has been no change in use of the property
and that the overnight stays should be considered as ancillary or incidental to
the use of the property as a café/restaurant. I have been directed to the
4

definitions of ancillary and incidental use included within the Glossary of the
Bridging Island Plan (2022) and interpretation of these terms within the UK.
Ancillary uses are entirely subservient to and dependent upon the main use
of the building and are relatively minor in scale and level of activity, whilst
incidental use relates to things associated with the principal use, which are
not considered ‘ancillary’ or as being a use for the primary function of the
site.
21. Whilst it is not currently operational, there has been no change in the
authorised use and primary function of the appeal property as a
café/restaurant. The plans accompanying the permission did not include
bedrooms or sleeping spaces or rest rooms for staff. Nor is there any
suggestion that the property would be occupied overnight. Therefore, I do
not consider that overnight stays and/or the introduction of beds to facilitate
these would meet the definition of ancillary or incidental use in this case.
Furthermore, as the café/restaurant is not operational, these overnight stays
are not subservient to or dependent upon the principal use of the building as
a café/restaurant. The appellant has suggested that the test for ancillary use
“is whether the activity is normally and reasonably expected as part of that
type of use.” For the reasons set out above, I do not see overnight stays within
the café/restaurant to be normally and reasonably expected as part of that
use.
22. Even were I to accept that an overnight stay for security reasons was
necessary, the nature and character of a stay by a family group is not the
same as that arising from having a professional Security Guard on site.
Overnight stays by family groups are more characteristic of a residential
setting.
23. During my site inspection I saw that the commercial kitchens are still in place.
I understand that there is a shower on site and there are toilets, (albeit these
may need repair). I saw no beds on site, although there is no dispute that
these were present at the time the Notice was served. The main internal
‘public’ area of the café/restaurant was set out in a manner that would be
more consistent of a domestic setting rather than a café/restaurant. Whilst
sofas and easy chairs are commonly found in some cafés, the limited number
and orientation of these combined with the table arrangement (a single, long
table, with few seating places) seems unsuited for a commercial café.
Additionally, there was a workstation, with computer. Whilst some cafés offer
access to computers there is usually more than one machine available. I also
saw various personal items including a display of birthday cards. In summary,
the arrangement appeared one of an open-plan living space, more typical of
a residential setting. As the café/ restaurant is not operational, this space
can only be used by, or with the permission of, the appellant.
24. I am not persuaded by the appellant’s statement that these alternative
layouts are ‘dressing’ to present the building to potential buyers. The
authorised use of the building is a café/restaurant and so there can be little
benefit in dressing it in a manner more typical of residential/ domestic use.
5

25. I accept that those who have stayed in the building have alternative ‘homes.’
However, that does not detract from the fact that the building was being used
as a residential unit at least at the time that the Notice was served and that
this falls outside the authorised use of the building. For the reasons set out
above, I conclude that the appeal under Ground (e) fails.
Other matters
26. The appellant has questioned the expediency of issuing the Notice, stating
that it is in the public interest to ensure that the building is protected from
vandalism, anti-social behaviour, and crime. However, there are alternative
ways of safeguarding the building.
27. The appellant has also queried whether the remedial steps required by the
Notice go beyond the Minister’s powers under Article 40. The Department has
referred me to the Murfitt principle (Murfitt v SSE, 1980). This established
that an Enforcement Notice can require removal of items that facilitate a
breach of planning control, even if those items in themselves would not
represent a breach of conditions. I am therefore satisfied that the
requirement to remove items contributing to residential use, including beds,
wardrobes/clothes and personal belongings associated with residential
occupation is within the Minister’s powers.
Recommendations
28. For the reasons set out above, I recommend that the appeal be dismissed and
the notice should stand.
Sue Bell
Inspector 8 December 2025
6
